title: Spore-forming bacteria survive inadequate autoclaving
vignette: An epidemiologic study is conducted after a sudden increase in the rate of severe postoperative infections in a rural hospital. It is determined that all the patients who developed the infection were operated on with surgical instruments sterilized by a specific autoclave machine. The machine was found to be inadequately sterilizing the equipment due to a malfunction limiting the temperature to 100 C (212 F) during the 15 minute cycle. These patients most likely developed infection from which of the following bacteria?

clues.note: Failed sterilization frames the question: something survived the process. The key is which organisms can withstand the residual conditions.

clues.text: limiting the temperature to 100 C (212 F)
clues.note: A proper autoclave reaches ~121-134 C under pressure; capping it at 100 C (boiling) is enough to kill vegetative cells but NOT bacterial spores. Spore-forming bacteria survive boiling.
Even 15 minutes at 100 C won't reliably destroy heat-resistant endospores. The surviving pathogens are therefore spore-formers (Bacillus and Clostridium).

whatMatters: An autoclave stuck at 100 C kills vegetative bacteria but NOT heat-resistant endospores. So the surviving pathogen is a spore-former — among the choices, Clostridium perfringens (Bacillus and Clostridium are the classic spore-forming genera). The others are non-spore-formers killed by boiling.
strategy: For sterilization-failure questions, ask 'what survives heat/chemicals/irradiation?' → bacterial ENDOSPORES (Bacillus, Clostridium). If the only spore-former among the options is a Clostridium or Bacillus, that's the answer; everything else is a vegetative organism killed at 100 C.

eliminations.label: Brucella — non-spore-former, killed by boiling
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Brucella is an intracellular gram-negative coccobacillus (unpasteurized dairy/animal contact, undulant fever). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it forms no spores and is killed by 100 C, so it wouldn't survive the cycle. ✅ Correct if: the scenario were undulant fever from unpasteurized dairy/livestock exposure.

eliminations.label: E. coli — non-spore-former
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: E. coli is a gram-negative enteric rod. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: vegetative gram-negatives are readily killed at 100 C; no endospores to survive. ✅ Correct if: the question concerned a non-sterility cause of gram-negative wound infection.

eliminations.label: Listeria — non-spore-former (cold-tolerant, not heat-spore)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Listeria monocytogenes grows at refrigeration temperatures and is killed by cooking/boiling. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: cold tolerance is not spore formation; 100 C destroys it. ✅ Correct if: the scenario were foodborne listeriosis from refrigerated deli/dairy in a pregnant/immunocompromised host.

eliminations.label: S. pyogenes — non-spore-former
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: Streptococcus pyogenes (group A strep) is a gram-positive coccus. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it forms no spores and is killed at 100 C; it can't survive the autoclave cycle. ✅ Correct if: the infection were a typical non-sterility surgical-site strep infection.

reasoning: Autoclave capped at 100 C kills vegetative cells but not endospores
Among the options, Clostridium perfringens is the spore-former (Bacillus/Clostridium)
mechanism: Bacterial endospores (formed by Bacillus and Clostridium) are dormant, dehydrated structures with a tough keratin-like coat and dipicolinic acid that confer extreme resistance to heat, desiccation, chemicals, and irradiation. A proper autoclave (121-134 C under pressure) destroys spores; a malfunction capping temperature at 100 C kills only vegetative cells, leaving spores viable to germinate and cause infection (e.g., gas gangrene from C. perfringens).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro > bacterial structure/sterilization: endospores (Bacillus, Clostridium), dipicolinic acid, resistance to heat/chemicals/irradiation; autoclave conditions (121 C, 15 psi). Cross-ref C. perfringens (gas gangrene).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Translate 'survived 100 C' into 'must be a spore-former,' then scan the options for the Bacillus/Clostridium. Identify the survival property before the species.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on endospore, vegetative cell, autoclave, dipicolinic acid, spore-forming genera? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Listeria (cold tolerance) and Brucella (intracellular) feel 'hardy,' but neither forms spores. Only the Bacillus/Clostridium endospore survives boiling — pick the Clostridium.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: If you knew spores survive but blanked on which bug, memorize the two spore-forming genera: Bacillus and Clostridium.

keywords.back: Dipicolinic acid + keratin-like coat, dehydrated dormant core

firstAid.detail: Micro > spores: Bacillus/Clostridium endospores, dipicolinic acid, resistance to heat/chemicals/radiation; autoclave (steam, 121 C) needed to kill spores.
firstAid.location: Micro > spores: Bacillus/Clostridium endospores, dipicolinic acid, resistance to heat/chemicals/radiation; autoclave (steam, 121 C) needed to kill spores.

firstAid.detail: Micro > Clostridia: C. perfringens (gas gangrene/myonecrosis, alpha-toxin lecithinase, food poisoning).
firstAid.location: Micro > Clostridia: C. perfringens (gas gangrene/myonecrosis, alpha-toxin lecithinase, food poisoning).

firstAid.topic: Listeria / Brucella / E. coli / S. pyogenes
firstAid.detail: Micro > these non-spore-formers' niches and why heat kills them — to contrast spore survival.
firstAid.location: Micro > these non-spore-formers' niches and why heat kills them — to contrast spore survival.

memoryHook: Only the SPORES survive a half-baked autoclave — and only Bacillus & Clostridium make them (here, Clostridium perfringens).
